title: Lavender, herb profile
subtitle: Lavandula angustifolia: the western tradition's most versatile nervine
excerpt: A materia medica profile of lavender, its traditional actions, therapeutic uses across the nervous system, skin, and musculoskeletal systems, preparation methods, and safety. The most widely used aromatic herb in the European tradition, documented from Hildegard of Bingen to the EMA.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), lavender entry. Culpeper, The English Physician (1652). Hildegard of Bingen, Physica (12th century), earliest European documentation of medicinal lavender use. EMA HMPC monograph on Lavandulae flos (2011).

content.text: True lavender, also called English or narrow-leaved lavender, is a woody scented plant from the western Mediterranean. It is the species used in cooking and home medicine. Two close cousins, spike lavender and lavandin, are common in cheap essential oils but are not the same plant and are not used inside the body. The dried flowers are the medicinal part. The main active is

, which makes up about a quarter to half of the essential oil. It acts on the brain's calming pathways, the same ones targeted by some prescription anxiety and sleep medicines, but at much lower strength and with no risk of dependence.

Lavender is a nervine: it calms the nervous system. It is also a mild sedative and a gentle anti-anxiety herb. It eases muscle spasm in both smooth muscle and the larger muscles you can feel. Used on the skin, it calms swelling and acts as a mild antiseptic. It also warms the skin enough to ease tension in cold or cramped muscles. The 12th-century writer Hildegard of Bingen used lavender for 'migraine and a pure knowledge of God', the first written European use as medicine. Old western herbals also list lavender as both a calming herb and a wound-cleaning herb on the skin. See the sources note for the older texts.

Internal use, as an infusion: mild anxiety, restlessness, trouble sleeping, mild low mood, and tension headache. External use: insect bites and stings as a cool compress; tight or sore muscles as a bath or compress; mild skin irritation as a compress or wash. Modern monographs confirm the traditional use for mild anxiety, restlessness, and sleep trouble, both as a tea and as a vapour. See the sources note for the modern reference.

Lavender has the strongest research record of any calming herb in the western tradition. A standardised lavender oil pill (Silexan, 80 mg a day) has been tested in several human trials. It is found to ease general anxiety and poor sleep at a much higher strength than a kitchen infusion. The kitchen infusion also has traditional status under modern monographs for mild anxiety and sleep trouble. The same biology that makes the pill work is at play in the cup of tea, just at a lower strength.

Infusion: 3, 5 g dried flowers per 250 ml water, steeped covered for 10 minutes. One to three cups daily. Bath: 30 g in 500 ml concentrated infusion, added to warm bath. Compress: 5, 10 g in 200 ml water, cooled, applied to skin. Tincture: 1:5 in 40% spirit, 2, 4 ml daily (less commonly made at home; commercial preparations are more consistent).

Lavender at normal infusion and topical doses is safe for most adults, including in pregnancy at culinary and low therapeutic concentrations. No significant drug interactions are documented at infusion doses. Lavender essential oil is not the same as the flower infusion, the oil should not be ingested. For topical use on sensitive skin, test on a small area first. See your doctor for anxiety, low mood, or insomnia that is persistent or significantly impairing.
